agent_outputs.raw: The benchmark comparison as presented is not valid for the migration decision. Two critical confounds exist. First, the baseline was evaluated with 30-day provisional labels while the challenger used 90-day matured labels. These are different fraud targets — 90-day labels include more resolved disputes and chargebacks. A model evaluated against a more complete label set will tend to look better regardless of true model quality. Second, the baseline was evaluated on Q1 and the challenger on Q2, a period after a merchant onboarding campaign changed transaction mix and geographies. Population shift between quarters confounds model quality with data distribution change. Additionally, a six-basis-point PR-AUC gain at 0.2% fraud prevalence is a small and potentially noisy estimate that requires confidence intervals to interpret. The temporal split design (by week) is sound and is not the issue. The label and quarter mismatch are the fundamental problems.

The fraud model comparison has two structural flaws that invalidate the claimed gain. One: the baseline and challenger used different label maturation windows (30 days vs. 90 days). More mature labels capture more fraud events, so a model scored against 90-day labels will show higher PR-AUC than the same model scored against 30-day labels, independent of model quality. Two: the evaluation quarters differ, and a merchant onboarding campaign changed the Q2 transaction mix. The six-basis-point PR-AUC difference cannot be interpreted as model improvement when the evaluation targets and populations both changed. A valid comparison requires both models re-evaluated on the same quarter with the same 90-day matured labels, with a paired bootstrap confidence interval for the PR-AUC difference.

This benchmark is broken in two ways. First, label maturation differs between baseline (30-day) and challenger (90-day) evaluations. This is a category error: you cannot compare PR-AUC values computed against different ground truths and call the difference a model quality signal. Second, different evaluation quarters with a known population shift (merchant onboarding campaign) means the data distributions differ. Both models need to be evaluated on the same quarter with the same label maturation standard. The temporal split by week is the right approach for fraud and should be preserved in the corrected evaluation. Without these fixes, the migration decision has no valid evidentiary basis. Critique wins.

synthesis: All three agents identify the label maturation mismatch and different evaluation quarters as the core flaws invalidating the comparison. All three agree the temporal split design is not the problem. All three recommend a common-quarter, common-label rerun with confidence intervals. Critique wins unanimously.

etd_constraint.condition: Re-evaluate both baseline and challenger on the same quarter using 90-day matured labels with week-forward temporal splits; compute paired bootstrap 95% CI for the PR-AUC difference
etd_constraint.supports_critique_if: Common-window PR-AUC gap falls below 0.003 or the 95% CI crosses zero
etd_constraint.supports_defense_if: Common-window gap is at least 0.006 with 95% CI excluding zero
etd_constraint.ambiguous_if: Gap is positive but below 0.006 with CI barely overlapping zero, suggesting a possible real but unconfirmed improvement

all_issues_raised: Label maturation mismatch: baseline 30-day vs. challenger 90-day (all three agents)
Different evaluation quarters with population shift (all three agents)
Missing confidence intervals for small delta (all three agents)
